name: Metamaterial Mechanisms
short_description: 3D-printed objects whose internal cellular microstructure makes the object itself a working machine, with mechanical function encoded directly in geometry.
long_description: Metamaterial Mechanisms, presented by Alexandra Ion and colleagues (including Patrick Baudisch) at UIST 2016, reconceives metamaterials as machines rather than passive materials. By arranging 3D-printed cellular grids that include specialized shearing cells, a single printed block can convert input motion into controlled macroscopic movement, demonstrated through examples such as a door latch that turns rotary handle motion into linear latch motion and a Jansen-style walker. It matters to physical AI and interaction design as a foundational tangible-fabrication technique: behavior lives in the structure and material itself rather than in motors, electronics, or computation, expanding the vocabulary of programmable matter and embodied interaction.
ai_note: Function is encoded purely in the printed cellular geometry; there is no AI, ML, electronics, or computation running inside the object.

images.caption: Teaser figure showing a 3D-printed metamaterial door latch whose cellular microstructure converts rotary handle motion into linear latch motion.
images.alt_text: A white 3D-printed block made of a fine grid of cells forming a door latch mechanism, with arrows indicating rotational input motion converting to linear output motion.

sources.title: Metamaterial Mechanisms
sources.publisher: Interactive Structures Lab, Carnegie Mellon University
